dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 responding for Cardiac Arrest at --Address Redacted--, [ADDRESS].
Hi, I'm Ben, an EMT with the rescue squad. What happened that led to the 911 call?
He had chest pain and shortness of breath. I saw him fall down after he started struggling for air.
Patient is unresponsive; GCS 3 noted in field report (E1, V1, M1).
Eyes remain closed; suspect no eye opening due to unresponsiveness.
No verbal response; unable to follow commands or respond.
No motor response reported; patient is completely unresponsive.
Partner, is the airway clear and patent?
Airway managed with an oropharyngeal airway and 7.0 oral endotracheal tube secured at the neck.
Apneic initially; ventilations via BVM via mask and then via tube at 10 breaths per minute.
Ventilations via BVM at 15LPM with endotracheal tube in place, as noted in field reports.
No peripheral pulses detected initially; rhythm is agonal PEA.
Carotid and femoral pulses noted during return of spontaneous circulation at 17:27, 17:37, and 17:46.
Central pulses at carotid and femoral absent at first, reported during ROSC.
Respiratory rate was 0 until assisted breathing with BVM and ETT.
Blood glucose was 81 mg/dL measured early in clinical evaluation.
Systolic and diastolic pressures started at 0/0; later 43/39, 76/48, and 95/66.
Oxygen saturation was 0% initially, but improved to 92, 99, and 95 following ROSC.
Pulse was 0 initially; later noted at 88, 125, and 116 during spontaneous circulation assessments.
4-lead ECG initially showed agonal PEA and idioventricular rhythm, later transitioning to sinus tachycardia.
Patient has a history of primary hypertension, atrial fibrillation, COPD, and is on renal dialysis.
Per report, patient had a recent central line placed in the left arm for dialysis access.
Current meds include ibuprofen, warfarin, diltiazem, albuterol, and others listed in the report.
Pain assessment not possible due to unresponsiveness; recorded as pain 0.
Intraosseous access established at the right humerus; included cravat across abdomen to prevent dislodgment.
Endotracheal tube a 7.0 size, placed with video laryngoscope after 7.5 size was too large.
Capnography used for airway confirmation; ETT secured after successful placement.
Administered epinephrine 1.0mg three times and 800 mL normal saline via IO line.
Manual CPR initiated at 17:06; switched to mechanical CPR via AutoPulse when ROSC episodes receded.
Patient was recently placed on a left arm central line for dialysis per the bystander's report.
4-lead continuous cardiac monitoring started at 17:10:52 for rhythm and pulse changes.
IO line was placed in the opposite arm (right), as left arm had a dialysis central line.
12-lead EKG findings included indeterminate axis, RBBB, and subendocardial injury.
Initial rhythm captured as pulseless electrical activity (PEA) or agonal rhythm.
12-lead ECG showed subendocardial injury, apical infarct features, and signs of RBBB.
Upon arrival, patient was moved directly to trauma bay by EMS and RFD.
AutoPulse used for mechanical CPR during ROSC regression per field notes.
PTA documentation indicates unresponsive; airway maintained per RFD prior to EMS arrival.
[COMPANY_NAME] selected for transport due to location and ongoing resuscitation needs.
Reeves sleeve used with seatbelts; patient secured with both arms in place.
All equipment, lines, and tubes visually verified during transport for prime functionality.
RFD members rode along, assisted with ventilation and condition checks in transit.
Vital signs reassessed during transport; HR of 125 and 116 with corresponding oxygen levels.
Handoff completed to ER nurse at [COMPANY_NAME] at 17:46.
Saline tubing torn mid-course; replaced and reattached to IO using pressure bag system.
Documents ROSC at 17:27, 17:37, and 17:46 after each epinephrine dose.
Airway suctioned with 18Fr and 14Fr to manage blood-tinged secretions per field notes.
No signs of visible agony in patient once breathing and circulation re-established.
Skin condition remains warm at neck, dry, no cyanosis throughout field report.
Patient fell as a result of difficulty breathing per eyewitness on scene.
Circulation initially absent; multiple ROSC episodes noted with variable BP and HR.
All lines and tubes reinforced; autosuction performed once during transport.
No STEMI found in all EKGs, but subendocardial injury and RBBB features identified.
Epinephrine administered in three rounds as per protocol with 10cc NS flushes.
Patient had a central line placed in left arm day of incident for dialysis session.
Pupils noted equally round and reactive at 3-4 mm per field report.
No urinary output reported or documented in field or during transport.
Documented allergies include lisinopril and fosinopril.
Current meds are listed in field notes and include anticoagulants and COPD agents.
All medications including epinephrine were administered via two-provider verification.
Blood noted in ETT from initial intubation; suctioned 5mL of blood-tinged fluid.
IO line CC reminded never to disconnect from tube; two EKG leads monitored visual.
Post-intubation, breath rate set to 10 per minute via ET tube with no signs of distress.
Respiration confirmed stable throughout transports; SpO2s remained at >90% post-ROSC.
Per protocol, epinephrine continues being used in PEA arrest at 1mg every 3-5 minutes.
Confirming airway via both capnography and monitoring visual lung rise.
Breath sounds confirmed in all fields bilaterally after suctioning the ETT.
Two RFD members were key in assisting with transport management inside the ambulance.
Destination set to [COMPANY_NAME] for trauma OR scenario possibly considering reperfusion.
Transport occurred between 17:46 and arrival estimated for prompt trauma bay admission.
Continuous saline IO preload monitored during multiple ROSC fluctuations in care.
Each epinephrine dose administered as per ISO Guidelines for continued ROSC episodes.
No additional agents administered during transports.
No impact on gastrointestinal tract; no reports of N&V during assisted respirations.
12-lead ECG repeatedly obtained including at 17:29:06 and 17:38:01 during transport prep.
All meds cross-checked prior to transfer and flushes verified if adhering to dosing protocol.
One round of intubation followed by BVM assist by RFD per report.
At 17:46, stabilizing efforts focused on monitoring and securing IO access for transfer.
Peripheral pulses entirely absent; only central pulses recovered during ROSC events.
Skin temperature reported warm at the neck; no mottling per field visit visuals.
Patient is on renal dialysis per history; however, no details of last treatment in report.
Skin condition clean, no rashes, no bites, and normal temperature throughout.
BVM, ETT, BVM with capnography, and AutoPulse all checked before arriving at the Trauma Bay.
Trauma bay staff noted patient's ongoing labile HR and BP per mid-transport vitals.
No blood pressure meds administered due to ongoing resuscitation and cardiac instability.
All drugs flushed upon any regrouping to prevent mixing of medications mid-procedure.
Reeves sleaved patient entirely with seats, monitored for IV/IO line functionality.
Trauma Bay nurses prepared to take over biosensors upon arrival at 17:46.
No reported fever or chills during transport or field notes.
Skin had no pallor or cyanosis reported by EMS or ER nurses per handover.
No exams performed on left arm for dialysis line; focused on securing right-side IO access.
All loops of ECG, pulse, respiratory were tasted and seconded to ER monitor prior arrival.
Bystander details that the patient collapsed and was unresponsive per scene description.
All resuscitation notes and log have been shared with ER over brief and protocol followed.
No demand stoppages or delays within rhythm during trip report to [COMPANY_NAME].
Ridealong reported no drop-off of EKG, pulse, or breath rate during trip.
At arrival, all iniatives transitioned to ER's charge; no meds after 17:46.
Full updated report notes were handed over to receiving facility as per protocol.
Documentation verified by shift; all vital signs and intervention logs are accurate.
All equipment and meds accounted for at drop off; carriage clear for disinfect.
